coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified NH2-MIL-125(Ti) acid-etching derived TiO2-based photocatalysts and efficient removal of high concentrations of dyes from wastewater under visible light

coredata.dc:description: TiO2 is considered one of the most promising photocatalysts for photocatalytic treatment of dye wastewater. However, TiO2 only responds to ultraviolet light, with a small specific surface area and weak photogenerated carrier separation ability, which limit its application in the treatment of high concentration dye wastewater. In this paper, 2-amino terephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) modified TiO2 photocatalyst (NH2BDCX-TiO2) were prepared for the first time through in situ acid-etching strategy of NH2-MIL-125(Ti), and then YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalysts were synthesized by loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on its surface. The results indicated that the NH2BDCX-TiO2 photocatalyst had excellent visible light response and enhanced adsorption capacity. In addition, the successful loading of CQDs on its surface effectively enhanced the separation of photogenerated carriers and further expanded the light absorption range of the composite photocatalyst to the near-infrared region. The adsorption and photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst for high concentration RhB solution (80 mg/L) reached 24.8 % and 87.1 %, respectively, which were 2.0 times and 435.5 times higher than commercial P25, respectively. Moreover, the optimum concentration of RhB solution, photocatalyst dosage and initial pH of RhB solution were also explored. Finally, based on active species capture experiments and energy band structure analysis, a possible mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of high concentration RhB solution by 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was also proposed. This work provided a new insight into the design of TiO2-based composite photocatalysts for the practical treatment of highly concentrated dye wastewater.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified NH2-MIL-125(Ti) acid-etching derived TiO2-based photocatalysts and efficient removal of high concentrations of dyes from wastewater under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 is considered one of the most promising photocatalysts for photocatalytic treatment of dye wastewater. However, TiO2 only responds to ultraviolet light, with a small specific surface area and weak photogenerated carrier separation ability, which limit its application in the treatment of high concentration dye wastewater. In this paper, 2-amino terephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) modified TiO2 photocatalyst (NH2BDCX-TiO2) were prepared for the first time through in situ acid-etching strategy of NH2-MIL-125(Ti), and then YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalysts were synthesized by loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on its surface. The results indicated that the NH2BDCX-TiO2 photocatalyst had excellent visible light response and enhanced adsorption capacity. In addition, the successful loading of CQDs on its surface effectively enhanced the separation of photogenerated carriers and further expanded the light absorption range of the composite photocatalyst to the near-infrared region. The adsorption and photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst for high concentration RhB solution (80 mg/L) reached 24.8 % and 87.1 %, respectively, which were 2.0 times and 435.5 times higher than commercial P25, respectively. Moreover, the optimum concentration of RhB solution, photocatalyst dosage and initial pH of RhB solution were also explored. Finally, based on active species capture experiments and energy band structure analysis, a possible mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of high concentration RhB solution by 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was also proposed. This work provided a new insight into the design of TiO2-based composite photocatalysts for the practical treatment of highly concentrated dye wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapidly development of the textile industry in many countries, large quantities of highly concentrated dye wastewater is widely discharged into the soil or water bodies, resulting in serious dye pollution [1–3]. Most dye molecules contain aromatic rings, which are highly toxic to living creatures and have mutagenic effects on humans and aquatic organisms [4]. In addition, the colour of the dyes affects the transmission of light in water, delaying the photosynthetic response of aquatic organisms and affecting their growth, thus destroying the ecological structure of the water [5]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a effective technology to treat high concentration dye wastewater.

At present, a variety of feasible methods, such as adsorption [6,7], biological treatment [8,9], electrochemical [10,11] and photocatalytic technologies [12], have been used to treat dye wastewater. However, the disadvantage of adsorption technology is that it cannot convert pollutants into harmless compounds [13,14], the tendency of secondary pollution from poisoning by-products in biological treatment technology[15] and the high energy consumption in electrochemical technology [16] limit the application of these three types of technology in the treatment of high concentration dye wastewater. Photocatalysis has become an ideal method for the removal of dye pollutants due to its advantages of converting pollutants into harmless compounds, no secondary pollution and low energy consumption [17]. TiO2 is considered one of the most promising photocatalysts due to its excellent photocatalytic activity, good chemical stability, low cost and non-toxicity [18,19]. Unfortunately, TiO2 still has some drawbacks that limit its practical application in the efficient treatment of high concentration dye wastewater. Firstly, the poor adsorption performance prevents it from rapidly adsorbing dye molecules in wastewater to enhance the light transmission rate, and the low light transmission rate seriously affects its photocatalytic activity in the treatment of high concentration dye wastewater [20]. Secondly, TiO2 has a narrow light absorption range, only responds to UV light, resulting in its low efficiency in utilizing solar energy [21]. Finally, the weak photo-generated carrier separation ability [22] of TiO2 leads to a rapid decrease in the number of effective carriers during degradation, which greatly limits its photocatalytic activity.

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), consisting of metal ions and organic ligands, are an emerging class of materials with a high specific surface area and easy functionalization [23]. In recent years, the application of MOFs as precursors for the derived preparation of modified metal oxide photocatalysts has gradually attracted the attention of researchers [23]. Among the MOFs, the MIL-125(Ti) has been widely used for the derived preparation of modified TiO2. However, in most reports, the 2-amino terephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) organic ligand was only regarded as precursors to prepare non-metallic elements modified TiO2. For example, He et al. utilized NH2-BDC organic ligand as carbon sources to prepare C-modified TiO2 through high-temperature pyrolysis, which improved its adsorption performance to a certain extent, but did not significantly improve the light absorption range [24]. Ismail et al. utilized NH2-BDC organic ligand of NH2-MIL-125(Ti) as the precursor of carbon and nitrogen sources to prepare C/N-TiO2 by high-temperature pyrolysis [25], which effectively enhanced photogenerated electron-hole separation, but the light absorption range was not significantly broadened. However, it has been reported that the NH2-BDC organic ligand can effectively broaden the light absorption range of TiO2 [26]. Not only that, Fan et al. used NH2-MIL-125(Ti) to adsorb and treat methylene blue (MB) in wastewater, and the results showed that the interactions provided by the benzene ring and amino functional groups in the organic ligand NH2-BDC greatly enhanced the adsorption of the dye molecules [27]. Therefore, we envision synthesizing TiO2 through NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) derivatization while retaining the NH2-BDC organic ligand, which is expected to improve the shortcomings of TiO2 and promote its effective treatment of high concentration dye wastewater.

In addition, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with excellent electron transport and upconversion fluorescence properties have been widely used to enhance photogenerated electron-hole separation and light absorption of photocatalysts [28,29]. For example, Xia et al. prepared the CQDs/Bi2WO6 composite photocatalysts, which exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity than pure Bi2WO6 for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, which was attributed to enhanced photogenerated electron-hole separation of the composite photocatalyst by CQDs [30]. Lin et al. prepared MIL-53(Fe)/CQDs composite photocatalysts using CQDs as co-catalysts. The results showed that CQDs could induce separation of photogenerated electron-hole from the internal structure of MOFs, which greatly improved their photocatalytic degradation activity of Cr(VI) [31]. Wang et al. prepared CQDs/NH2-MIL-125(Ti) composite photocatalysts and showed that CQDs not only inhibited photogenerated electron-hole recombination in NH2-MIL-125(Ti), but also greatly broadened the light absorption range and improved the efficiency of its use of light [32]. Moreover, CQDs have the advantages of low cost, good chemical stability and simple preparation methods [33]. Therefore, CQDs are ideal materials to be used to further increase the activity of photocatalysts.

In the work, the TiO2 photocatalyst modified by 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2BDCX-TiO2) was prepared by in situ acid-etching strategy of a metal organic framework (MOF)-sacrificed, followed by the preparation of YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalysts loaded with carbon quantum dots by solvent deposition (See Scheme 1). The characterization results confirmed that NH2BDC10-TiO2 photocatalyst has excellent visible light response and enhanced adsorption capacity. In addition, the successful loading of CQDs on its surface effectively enhances the photogenerated electron-hole separation capability, while further improving the light absorption intensity and light response range of the composite photocatalyst. The 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst showed good removal efficiency when applied to the treatment of highly concentrated RhB solution. In addition, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of highly concentrated RhB solution by 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst was also explored. This work provided a new insight into the design of TiO2-based composite photocatalysts for the practical treatment of highly concentrated dye wastewater.

Methanol (MeOH, 99.7 %) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5 %) were purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Butyl titanate (TBT, 97 %), rhodamine B (RhB, AR), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.7 %), benzoquinone (BQ, 98 %) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 98 %) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Amino terephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, 98 %) and melamine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Glucose (C6H12O6, 99.7 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.7 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 98 %) and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99.5 %) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Wholesale Company.

NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was synthesized according to the reported solvothermal method in the literature[34]. Specifically, 27 mL of DMF and 3 mL of MeOH were mixed in a beaker, and then 1.36 g of 2-aminoterephthalic acid was added to the mixed solution for sonication until a clear solution was formed. Then 0.51 mL of butyl titanate was added to the clear solution for sonication for 30 min before transferring to an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacting at 150 °C for 48 h. The collected samples were washed several times with DMF and methanol and then dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. The obtained NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was designated as NM(Ti).

NH2BDCX-TiO2 photocatalyst was obtained based on the following acid-etching strategy. Specifically, a solution of acetic acid at a concentration of X mol/L (X = 5, 10, 15 and 20) was prepared in a beaker, then 200 mg of NM(Ti) was added to the acetic acid solution for sonication, and after sonication for 1 h, the above mixture was loaded into a reactor containing polytetrafluoroethylene liner and reacted at 90 °C for 12 h. Finally, the target material was obtained by centrifugal washing and drying, and the target material was named NH2BDCX-TiO2, where X referred to the concentration of acetic acid. The prepared composite photocatalysts were NH2BDC5-TiO2, NH2BDC10-TiO2, NH2BDC15-TiO2 and NH2BDC20-TiO2, respectively.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared according to the alkali-assisted sonication method. Specifically, 9.0 g of glucose was homogeneously dissolved in 50 mL of deionised water, followed by 50 mL of NaOH (1 mol/L) solution and stirred to achieve complete mixing. The colourless solution was then sonicated for 120 min at room temperature to obtain the yellow-brown solution. Finally, a dialysis bag (MWCO 3000) was used to further purify the CQDs stock solution.

YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst was prepared according to the solvent deposition method. Specifically, 100 mg of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and Y mL (Y = 0.16, 0.31, 0.47, and 0.62) of CQDs solution (16.03 g/L) were uniformly dispersed in 30 mL of deionised water and immersed in a low temperature thermostatic bath at 90 °C for 3 h. Finally the sample was collected and named YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, where Y was the mass of CQDs percentage. The prepared photocatalysts were 2.5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, 7.5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 10CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, respectively.

The crystal structure of the samples was recorded by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D8-FOCUS, Bruker, Germany) using a Cu-Kα radiation monochromatic detector with a scanning range of 5°−50° and a scanning speed of 8° min−1. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT IR Tracer 100, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to identify possible chemical bonds, molecular structure and functional groups in the samples. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7100F, Japan) was used to examine surface morphology and microstructure. The morphology and structure of the photocatalyst was analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL F200, Japan). X-ray photo-electron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) measurements were used to analyse the surface properties and composition of the samples. Photoluminescence spectra (PL, RF-600 SHIMADZU, Japana) were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The optical properties of all samples were studied with a U.K. PE Lambda 1050+ UV-Vis Near Infrared Absorption Spectrometer (Lambda 1050, PerkinElmer, USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent measurements were performed using an electrochemical station equipped with a representative three-electrode system (CHI660D, Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China). A Pt electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. The electrochemical tests were carried out with an aqueous 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte and a 300 W xenon lamp as the light source.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was assessed by photocatalytic degradation of high concentrations of RhB under visible light irradiation. A 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm filter was used as the visible light source. In general, a 20 mg photocatalyst was added to 100 mL of RhB solution at a concentration of 80 mg/L and stirred in the dark for 60 min to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The reaction beaker of RhB solution was kept 15 cm away from the light source. An amount of sample solution was removed from the reactor every 20 min, the photocatalyst was filtered and the RhB concentration was measured at a maximum absorption wavelength of 554 nm using an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, UV-5500).

The principle of determination is based on salicylic acid capture·OH produces 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid and 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, which have maximum absorption at a wavelength of 510 nm.·OH shows a linear relationship with 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid and 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, so the higher the absorbance measured at 510 nm, the more·OH is proved to be produced by the system.

For the determination of·O2 -, Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) was choose as a probe. Under the action of·O2 -, NBT was reduced to form the insoluble blue Diformazan and its maximum absorption peak appears at 560 nm. Furthermore, the production amount of formazan was linearly related to the amount of·O2 -, so we can utilize this relationship to determine·O2 - quantitatively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the crystal structure of NM(Ti), NH2BDCX-TiO2, YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 and P25, the prepared samples were characterized using XRD. As shown in Fig. 1a, the characteristic diffraction peaks of NM(Ti) at 2θ= 6.84°, 9.79°, 11.69° and 15.42° were observed, corresponding to the (101), (002), (211) and (220) crystal planes of NH2-MIL-125(Ti), respectively, which was consistent with the reported literature and indicated the successful preparation of NM(Ti) [35]. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ= 25.28°, 37.80°, and 48.05° in the XRD pattern of P25 corresponded to the (101), (004) and (200) crystallographic planes of anatase TiO2, respectively, while the other characteristic diffraction peaks were attributed to rutile TiO2 [21]. Compared to the XRD pattern of NM(Ti) and P25, the XRD pattern of NH2BDCX-TiO2 showed the characteristic diffraction peaks associated with NM(Ti) gradually decreased in intensity until they disappeared as the concentration of acetic acid increased, while the characteristic diffraction peaks associated with anatase TiO2 gradually appeared and increased in intensity. This indicated that the crystal structure of NM(Ti) was destroyed during the acid-etching process as the concentration of acetic acid increased, and that the process was accompanied by the formation of anatase TiO2. This was attributed to the fact that NM(Ti) was unstable under acidic conditions [36] and the relatively weak binding strength of the titanium oxide clusters to the linker [37], resulting in the easy hydrolytic ageing of the titanium oxide clusters to form nano-TiO2 [38,39] and the titanium dioxide clusters were mainly transformed into anatase TiO2 at low pH values [40]. Fig. 1b showed the XRD pattern of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2. The same diffraction broad peaks of anatase TiO2 as NH2BDC10-TiO2 were also observed in the XRD pattern of YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2. A new diffraction peak at 2θ= 27° appeared in the XRD results of YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, which corresponded to the (002) crystal plane diffraction peak of CQDs [41], indicating the successful loading of CQDs and also proving that it would not damage the overall structure of NH2BDC10-TiO2.

The surface functional groups of NM(Ti), NH2BDCX-TiO2, NH2-BDC, P25 + NH2BDC, NH2-BDC, P25, CQDs and YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were analyzed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2a, the prepared NM(Ti) showed characteristic vibration absorption bands of Ti-O-Ti in titanium-oxygen clusters of 425–775 cm−1 [42]. The absorption peaks at 1380 cm−1, 1434 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 were attributed to the C-N stretching vibration peak, the -OH symmetric stretching vibration peak and the CO stretching vibration peak of the aromatic amine, respectively [34], and the absorption broad peaks between 3200 and 3600 cm−1 were attributed to the -NH2 stretching vibration peak [43]. These characteristic peaks were similar to those of the NH2-BDC sample and it could be determined that the functional groups were all from the NH2-BDC structure. The broad peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of P25 in the 400–800 cm−1 range were ascribed to O-Ti-O bonds in the anatase phase TiO2 structure and were clearly different from the characteristic vibration absorption bands of the titanium-oxygen clusters in NM(Ti) [44]. The FT-IR spectra of NH2BDCX-TiO2 showed the broad peaks in the range 400–800 cm−1 consistent with TiO2 and the absorption peaks in the range 1380–1625 cm−1 consistent with NH2-BDC in NM(Ti), suggesting that not only TiO2 was produced but also NH2-BDC was retained after acid-etching of NM(Ti). In addition, an absorption peak at 1690 cm−1 was observed in the FT-IR spectrum of the NH2BDCX-TiO2 sample, which may be due to the interaction between TiO2 and the organic ligand NH2-BDC [45]. Whereas the stretching vibration peaks of CO in the FT-IR plots of P25 + NH2BDC, a sample physically mixed with P25 and NH2-BDC, were not shifted, which again confirmed the interaction between TiO2 and the organic ligand NH2-BDC in the NH2BDCX-TiO2 prepared by acid-etching. As shown in Fig. 2b, the absorption peaks at 1085 cm−1, 1434 cm−1, 1625 cm−1 and 2959 cm−1 were clearly observed in the FT-IR plots of the prepared CQDs, which were attributed to the stretching vibration peak of C-O, the symmetric stretching vibration peak of -OH, the stretching vibration peak of CO and the stretching vibration peak of C-H, respectively [46]. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of the NH2BDC10-TiO2 and YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, both samples showed characteristic absorption peaks associated with TiO2 and NH2-BDC, with the difference that YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 displayed the characteristic absorption peaks at 1085 cm−1 and 2959 cm−1 belonged to CQDs and the peak intensity increased with the loading of CQDs, indicating once again that CQDs were successfully loaded on the surface of NH2BDC10-TiO2 by solvent deposition. Combining the above XRD and FT-IR results, it had been proven that NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was derived to generate TiO2 while retaining the ligand NH2-BDC through acid etching method, as well as the successful loading of CQDs on its surface, thus the YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst was prepared successfully.

For understanding the microstructure of the samples, NM(Ti), NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were characterized by SEM and TEM. The SEM images of NM(Ti) and NH2BDC10-TiO2 were showed in Fig. 3a and b. The results in Fig. 3a showed that NM(Ti) presented a decahedral structure with a smooth surface, uniform distribution and a particle size of approximately 800 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, after acid-etching, the originally regular shaped particles with a large particle size were transformed into irregular shaped aggregates with a particle size of around 100 nm, which also confirmed that the morphology structure of NM(Ti) was destroyed after acid-etching. For the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst, the presence of CQDs could not be clearly observed in the SEM results due to the small size of the CQDs. Therefore TEM characterization of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3c, CQDs were uniformly dispersed and deposited on the surface of NH2BDC10-TiO2. Fig. 3d was an enlarged view in Fig. 3c, where two different lattice stripes were clearly showed. A lattice spacing of 0.35 nm corresponded to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 [47], while a lattice spacing of 0.228 nm corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [48]. Furthermore, it could also be observed in Fig. 3d that the CQDs were well dispersed on the NH2BDC10-TiO2 surface, again confirming the successful loading of CQDs on the surface of NH2BDC10-TiO2.

In order to determine the elemental state and surface composition of NM(Ti), NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, XPS characterization of the samples was carried out. Fig. 4a showed the full XPS spectra of these three samples, which all displayed C 1s, N 1s, Ti 2p and O 1s peaks, indicating the presence of C, N, Ti and O elements. Compared with NM (Ti), the full spectrum of NH2BDC10-TiO2 showed a significant decrease in the intensity of C1s and N1s peaks, which was attributed to the loss of some organic ligand during the acid etching process of NM(Ti). Differently, the full spectrum of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 showed an increase in the intensity of the C 1s peak and a decrease in the Ti 2p peak compared with NH2BDC10-TiO2. This was attributed to the successful loading of CQDs on the NH2BDC10-TiO2 surface. In Fig. 4b, comparing with the C 1s energy spectrum of NM(Ti), it was observed that the intensity of CC peak in the C 1s energy spectrum of NH2BDC10-TiO2 was obviously weakened, which might be attributed to the loss of some organic ligand during acid etching of NM(Ti). While the COO- peak showed a shift towards the low binding energy, which might be attributed to the breakage of coordination bonds between the titanium-oxygen cluster and the linker during acid etching of NM(Ti).[49,50]. The C 1s energy spectrum of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 showed the appearance of the C-O peak at 285.2 eV, which corresponded to the hydroxyl functional group on the surface of CQDs[20], which also confirmed the successful loading of CQDs onto the surface of NH2BDC10-TiO2. As a result, the peak intensity of CC and COO- in the C1s spectrum of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 increased compared to NH2BDC10-TiO2. In Fig. 4c, the presence of the -NH2 peak at 399.5 eV and the -N+ peak at 402.6 eV in the energy spectrum of the N 1s of NM(Ti) correlated with the -NH2 functional group of NH2-BDC in NM(Ti) and the outward extension or protrusion of the N element in the -NH2 functional group into the MOF cavity, respectively [51]. Comparing with the N 1s energy spectrum of NM(Ti), the disappearance of the -N+ peak at 402.6 eV and the appearance of a new peak at 400.9 eV in the energy spectrum of NH2BDC10-TiO2 was observed. The disappearance of the -N+ peak confirmed the disruption of the NM(Ti) crystal structure after acid etching. In addition, the new peak at 400.9 eV was attributed to the Ti-O-N bond [52,53], and the N element in NH2BDC10-TiO2 photocatalyst originated from the amino functional group of the organic ligand NH2-BDC, so an interaction between NH2-BDC and TiO2 could be identified. The N1s energy spectrum of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was consistent with that of NH2BDC10-TiO2, but the intensity of its N peak was reduced due to the surface loading of CQDs. In Fig. 4d, the -OH peak at 532.6 eV from the titanium oxygen cluster in the O 1s energy spectrum of NH2BDC10-TiO2 almost disappeared compared with NM(Ti), which was due to the conversion of the titanium oxygen cluster in the structure to TiO2 after acid-etching. In addition, the weak displacement of the Ti-O and CO peaks in NH2BDC10-TiO2 compared with NM(Ti) again illustrated the interaction between NH2-BDC and TiO2. Compared to the O 1 s energy spectrum of NH2BDC10-TiO2, the C-OH peak at 532.7 eV in 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was derived from the hydroxyl functional group on the surface of CQDs. In Fig. 4e, NM(Ti), NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 all showed characteristic peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 around at 458.7 eV and 464.4 eV, implying that Ti (IV) was present in all three samples [24]. The Ti peaks of both NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were significantly displaced compared to NM(Ti) due to the conversion of Ti from NM(Ti) to TiO2. In brief, the XPS characterization results confirmed NM(Ti) had been successfully converted to NH2BDC10-TiO2 and the ligand NH2-BDC interacted with TiO2. It was also confirmed that CQDs were successfully loaded on the surface of NH2BDCX-TiO2. This result was consistent with the above FT-IR, XRD, SEM and TEM results.

In order to demonstrate that NH2-BDC modified TiO2 could effectively overcome the drawback that TiO2 only responded to UV light, and that the loading of CQDs could further enhance the light absorption of NH2BDC10-TiO2, the prepared samples were characterized by UV–vis DRS. Fig. 5a displayed the UV–vis DRS spectra of NM(Ti), P25, NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, from which it could be seen that the optical absorption edge of NH2BDC10-TiO2 was broadened to 550 nm, indicating that the modification of NH2-BDC could greatly enhance the responsiveness of TiO2 to visible light, even to the near-infrared region. For 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, the result indicated the introduction of CQDs could indeed further enhance light absorption range and intensity of NH2BDC10-TiO2. In addition, PL emission spectra, EIS, and transient photocurrent were completed to investigate the separation and migration ability of photogenerated electron-hole in the samples. PL emission spectra of samples were first tested at an excitation wavelength of 250 nm. For PL results, the weaker the PL intensity, the higher the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole. As shown in Fig. 5b, the PL intensities of NM(Ti), NH2BDC10-TiO2 and YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were in the following order: 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 < 2.5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 < 7.5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 < 10CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 < NH2BDC10-TiO2 < NM(Ti). The results illustrated that the separation of photogenerated electron-hole was effectively improved by loading CQDs in NH2BDC10-TiO2 photocatalyst. For the EIS test, a smaller radius meant a lower migration resistance of photogenerated electrons and a higher ability to migrate photogenerated electrons. Therefore, in the results of the EIS in Fig. 5c, YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 exhibited a better photogenerated electron migration ability compared to NH2BDC10-TiO2, and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 possessed the strongest photogenerated electrons migration ability. Finally, the transient photocurrent also demonstrated similar results. As shown in Fig. 5d, the NH2BDC10-TiO2 loaded with CQDs further increased the photocurrent density. Combining with the results of PL spectra, electrochemical impedance spectra and transient photocurrent, it was confirmed that the loading of CQDs effectively improved the separation and migration ability of photogenerated electron-hole.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the photocatalytic activity of samples and its influencing factors
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 showed the photocatalytic removal results of high concentration RhB solution by NM(Ti), NH2BDCx-TiO2, P25 and P25 + NH2BDC. Firstly, combined with the adsorption and degradation efficiency in Table 1, it could be seen that the prepared NH2BDCX-TiO2 by acid-etching outperformed the P25 + NH2BDC sample prepared by physical mixing in terms of adsorption and photodegradation efficiency, which was first due to the fact that NH2BDCX-TiO2 retained a large specific surface area of NM (Ti), and second contributed to the improved light absorption based on the tight junctions between NH2-BDC and TiO2. Moreover, it was clear that NH2BDC10-TiO2 exhibited the best photocatalytic removal efficiency. However, compared with NM(Ti), although the adsorption efficiency of NH2BDC10-TiO2 increased by 8.6 %, the photodegradation efficiency only increased by 6.2 %. The increase in adsorption efficiency might be attributed to the interactions provided by amino functional group in NH2BDC10-TiO2 and the benzene ring in RhB molecule. While the small increase in photodegradation efficiency indicated that NH2BDC10-TiO2 had the low photocatalytic activity for the high concentration RhB, which might be due to the weak photogenerated electron-hole separation. Therefore, further modification of NH2BDC10-TiO2 photocatalyst was required to enhance photocatalytic activity.

Combined with the results in Fig. 7a and Table 2, comparative analysis of the adsorption and degradation capacity of NH2-BDC10-TiO2, YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, and P25 for high concentration RhB showed that loading CQDs on the surface of NH2BDC10-TiO2 could effectively improve its low photocatalytic activity in the degradation stage, which was due to the excellent photogenerated electron capture and migration capabilities of CQDs, thus inhibiting the recombination their of photogenerated electron-hole. And the YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst had the best degradation efficiency when the loading of CQDs was 5 %. However, the YCQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalysts all showed a decrease in adsorption efficiency, which could be attributed to the deposition of CQDs on NH2BDC10-TiO2 reducing the specific surface area of the composite. Fortunately, 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 still had an ideal adsorption efficiency of 28 %, which did not affect 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 as an ideal material for treating high concentration RhB wastewater. In Fig. 7b, the removal efficiency of the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst were investigated for different concentrations of RhB solution. The best removal efficiency of the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst was achieved when the RhB solution was 80 mg/L. It was worth noting that when the RhB solution was 60 mg/L, excessive adsorption would make the RhB dye molecules easily and strongly adsorb onto the active centre of the composite photocatalyst, thus greatly reducing its photocatalytic activity. When the RhB solution concentration was higher than 80 mg/L, the insufficient adsorption capacity of the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst would lead to a decrease in the light transmission of the composite photocatalyst, thus affecting the light absorption of the composite photocatalyst and reducing its photocatalytic activity. Therefore, 80 mg/L RhB solution was used as the optimum experimental concentration. In Fig. 7c, the effect of the dosage of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst on its photocatalytic activity was also investigated. The highest removal efficiency was achieved at a dosage of 20 mg. When the dosage was higher than 20 mg, the adsorption amount increased substantially, but the photocatalytic degradation efficiency decreased significantly, which was caused by the excessive adsorption capacity. At a dose of 10 mg, the transmittance was reduced due to the lower adsorption capacity, resulting in an eventual reduction of the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the optimum dosage for the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst was 20 mg. In Fig. 7d, the effect results of the initial pH of the solution on the removal of RhB of the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst showed that the best removal efficiency was achieved at pH= 7. Notably, the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst exhibited lower adsorption efficiency and photocatalytic activity under both acidic and alkaline conditions. It was because, under acidic conditions, the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst exhibited strong electrostatic repulsion towards RhB cations due to the positive surface charge, which prevented the adsorption of RhB molecules and led to a decrease in photocatalytic activity [53]. Under alkaline conditions, the carboxyl group on the RhB molecule was dissociated, which weakened the interaction between RhB and the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst, also leading to a reduction in photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of high-concentration RhB
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the possible mechanism of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation of high concentration RhB solution under visible light, the main active species were evaluated through active species capture experiments. As shown in Fig. 8a, the active species hydroxyl radical (·OH), superoxide radical (·O2 -) and hole (h+) were captured by isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and EDTA, respectively. The addition of IPA, BQ and EDTA significantly reduced the photocatalytic degradation efficiency by 34.8 %, 44.1 % and 55.9 %, respectively. To better understand the reaction kinetics of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst on high concentration RhB in the capture experiment, its degradation kinetics curve was plotted based on a pseudo first-order model (ln (C/C0)=kt. As shown in Fig. 8b, the rate constant k values could be calculated from the slope and intercept of the linear plot, and the order of the rate constant k values was: None (k = 0.01357) > IPA (k = 0.00466) > BQ (k = 0.00221) > EDTA (k = 0.00139). The results showed that the order of the main active species affecting the photocatalytic degradation of RhB solution was h+ >·O2 - > ·OH.

To further confirm the photocatalytic degradation mechanism, the energy band structure of the photocatalysts were investigated using Tauc plots, XPS valence band spectra and Mott-Schottky plots. As shown in Fig. 9a, the bandgap values of P25, NM(Ti), NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were estimated by Tauc plots, which showed that the bandgap value of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 (Eg=1.68 eV) composite photocatalysts was smaller than those of P25 (Eg=3.10 eV), NM(Ti) (Eg=2.76 eV) and NH2BDC10-TiO2(Eg=2.40 eV). This was in agreement with the DRS characterization results and again confirmed that the loading of CQDs further enhanced the light absorption range of the 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst. However, the band gap value could not be determined only by the results of the Tauc plot, as the darker colour of the sample after loading with CQDs had an effect on the light absorption. Therefore, the valence and conduction bands of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were further characterized by XPS valence band spectra and Mott-Schottky plots. In Fig. 9b, the maximum valence band (VB) values of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were 2.30 and 2.15 eV, respectively. In addition, the Mott-Schottky plots for both samples at 1.0 kHz frequency were given in Fig. 9c. The positive slope confirmed the n-type semiconductor characteristics of the two samples. The flat bands potential of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were −0.30 and −0.40 eV, respectively. It was known that the conduction band minimum (CB) of n-type semiconductors was negative by 0–0.1 eV compared to the flat band potential. The CB bands of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were determined equal to the flat band potentials to be −0.30 and −0.40 eV, respectively. Combined with the XPS valence band spectra and Mott-Schottky plots, the band gaps (Eg) of the two samples were obtained according to the formula (Eg=EVB-ECB) and the band gaps of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were 2.60 and 2.55 eV, respectively. It was consistent with the results of Tauc plot of NH2BDC10-TiO2 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 possessed smaller band gap. Based on the above results, the energy band structure of NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were plotted in Fig. 9d. It was clear that after loading CQDs on the surface of NH2BDC10-TiO2, both the valence and conduction band positions of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 shifted negatively, implying that it possessed weaker ability to generate·OH- and stronger ability to generate·O2 -. In addition, in order to better compared and studied the formation and changes of radicals in NH2BDC10-TiO2 and 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 under illumination, the quantitative capture experiments on·OH- and·O2 - for the above two photocatalysts were conducted. The experimental results in Fig. 9e and f indicated that compared with NH2BDC10-TiO2, the ability to generate·OH- was weakened for 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2, while the ability to generate·O2 - was enhanced. It was consistent with the band structure analysis results.

Based on the results of the active species capture experiment and energy band structure, a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of high concentration RhB solution by 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was proposed in Fig. 10. Specifically, a large number of RhB molecules were first adsorbed on the surface of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst under dark reaction conditions and reached the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, under visible light irradiation, 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalyst produced photogenerated electrons and holes, where the photogenerated electrons were excited to the CB position and transferred to CQDs based on its properties of capturing electrons, which would contributed to the efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole. In contrast, the photogenerated holes (h+) remained in the VB position. In addition, the CB potential of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 was −0.40 eV, which was more negative than the reduction potential of O2/·O2 - (−0.33 eV vs NHE), this allowed the photogenerated electrons on the CB of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 to react with the O2 adsorbed on the surface of the composite photocatalyst to form·O2 -. However, the VB potential of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 (2.15 eV vs NHE) was slightly higher than the oxidation potential of OH-/·OH (1.99 eV vs NHE), and the OH- in the reaction system could be oxidized by h+ to form a small amount of·OH. Therefore, RhB molecules adsorbed on the surface of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were oxidized and degraded by h+,·O2 - and·OH. Additionally, it was also inferred that the main active species of 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation of high concentration RhB solution were·O2 - and h+, followed by·OH.

In summary, NH2BDCX-TiO2 photocatalysts were first prepared by a metal organic framework (MOF)-sacrificed in situ acid-etching strategy, and then 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalysts were prepared by solvent deposition. The results showed that the NH2BDCx-TiO2 photocatalyst had excellent visible light response and enhanced adsorption ability. In addition, the successful loading of CQDs on its surface effectively enhanced the photogenerated electron-hole separation and also further enhanced the light absorption of the composite photocatalyst up to the near-infrared light region. Compared with commercial P25, 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 composite photocatalysts achieved 24.8 % adsorption rate and 87.1 % photocatalytic degradation efficiency for high concentration RhB solution, which was 2.0 times and 435.5 times higher than that of P25, respectively. Furthermore, the results of active species capture experiments and energy band structure analysis demonstrated that the main active species for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB at high concentrations by 5CQDs/NH2BDC10-TiO2 were·O2 - and h+, followed by·OH. This work provided a new insight into the design of TiO2-based composite photocatalysts for the practical treatment of highly concentrated dye wastewater. This study also had significant theoretical and practical application value for the prevention and treatment of water environmental pollution in China.

Jiating Shi: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Visualization. Chenke Ju: Validation, Investigation. Tiantian Yang: Validation, Formal analysis.
